perturbation.original_text: Distributor agrees to use commercially reasonable efforts to introduce, promote the sale of, and obtain orders for the Products and Services in connection with Multiple LINAC or Multi-Modality Purchases, including, without limitation, including the Products and Services in each of Distributor's 4 Oncology Care Systems price book and sales operation system, such that all of Distributor's sales representatives can access quotations for Products and Services at least as easily as all other systems then available for purchase from Distributor.
perturbation.changed_text: Distributor will endeavor to introduce and promote the Products and Services in connection with Multiple LINAC or Multi-Modality Purchases, potentially including the Products and Services in Distributor's Oncology Care Systems price book and sales operation system, allowing Distributor's sales representatives to access quotations for Products and Services in a manner they deem appropriate.
perturbation.explanation: The modified text weakens the obligation from 'commercially reasonable efforts' to 'endeavor', and makes the inclusion in the price book and sales operation system optional by using 'potentially including'. The phrase 'in a manner they deem appropriate' introduces subjectivity, making the promotional efforts less enforceable.
perturbation.contradicted_law: California Business and Professions Code Section 17200
perturbation.law_explanation: California Business and Professions Code Section 17200 prohibits unfair competition. By making the promotional efforts vague and discretionary, the distributor could engage in practices that, while technically not breaches of contract, could be considered unfair competition if they disadvantage Accuray in the market or mislead customers, contradicting the intent of the law to ensure fair market practices.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: California Code, BPC 17200 skip to content home accessibility FAQ feedback sitemap login x Quick Search: Bill Number Bill Keyword Home Bill Information California Law Publications Other Resources My Subscriptions My Favorites California Law >> >> Code Section Code Section Code: Select Code CONS BPC CIV CCP COM CORP EDC ELEC EVID FAM FIN FGC FAC GOV HNC HSC INS LAB MVC PEN PROB PCC PRC PUC RTC SHC UIC VEH WAT WIC Article: Section: Code: Select Code All BPC CIV CCP COM CORP EDC ELEC EVID FAM FIN FGC FAC GOV HNC HSC INS LAB MVC PEN PROB PCC PRC PUC RTC SHC UIC VEH WAT WIC Section: Keyword(s): Code Search Text Search Up^ << Previous Next >> cross-reference chaptered bills PDF | Add To My Favorites Search Phrase: Code Text Business and Professions Code - BPC DIVISION 7. GENERAL BUSINESS REGULATIONS [16000 - 18107] ( Division 7 added by Stats. 1941, Ch. 61. ) PART 2. PRESERVATION AND REGULATION OF COMPETITION [16600 - 17365] ( Part 2 added by Stats. 1941, Ch. 526. ) CHAPTER 5. Enforcement [17200 - 17210] ( Chapter 5 added by Stats. 1977, Ch. 299. ) 17200. As used in this chapter, unfair competition shall mean and include any unlawful, unfair or fraudulent business act or practice and unfair, deceptive, untrue or misleading advertising and any act prohibited by Chapter 1 (commencing with Section 17500) of Part 3 of Division 7 of the Business and Professions Code. (Amended by Stats. 1992, Ch. 430, Sec. 2. Effective January 1, 1993.)
perturbation.justification: The original contract requires 'commercially reasonable efforts' to promote products, while the modified text only requires Distributor to 'endeavor', and makes inclusion of the products in the sales systems 'potentially including' rather than mandatory. The original provides that the sales reps have access to quotations 'at least as easily as all other systems,' while the changed text provides it will be done 'in a manner they deem appropriate.' The LLM explanation and perturbation explanation both suggest the modifications create a vagueness and discretionary power that did not previously exist. The law snippet BPC 17200 defines unfair competition as 'any unlawful, unfair or fraudulent business act or practice'. By making the promotional efforts less stringent and more discretionary, the distributor could potentially engage in unfair practices, contradicting the law's intent to ensure fair market practices. Therefore, the altered language creates a contradiction because it weakens the obligation enough to where unfair practices could occur.

perturbation.original_text: Accuray will provide a warranty to each Customer that the Products will be free from material defects and perform substantially in accordance with the written Specifications provided by Accuray as reflected in the regulatory clearance at the time of sale for a period of one (1) year following Installation of the Products at Customer's facility, but not to exceed eighteen (18) months following shipment of such Products to Distributor ("Warranty Period").
perturbation.changed_text: Accuray will provide a warranty to each Customer that the Products will, to a reasonable extent, be free from defects and perform in accordance with the Specifications for a duration that Accuray considers suitable following Installation of the Products at Customer's facility.
perturbation.explanation: The modified text replaces 'material defects' with 'to a reasonable extent, be free from defects', making the standard subjective. It also removes the specific warranty period, replacing it with 'a duration that Accuray considers suitable', giving Accuray complete discretion over the warranty period.
perturbation.law_explanation: UCC § 2-313 creates express warranties by affirmation, promise, description, or sample. The original text establishes a clear warranty. The modified text, by being vague about the extent of defects covered and the duration of the warranty, fails to provide the clear affirmation required by the UCC, potentially denying the customer the protections afforded by an express warranty under state law implementing the UCC.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: § 2-313. Express Warranties by Affirmation, Promise, Description, Sample. | Uniform Commercial Code | US Law | LII / Legal Information Institute Please help us improve our site! × No thank you § 2-313. Express Warranties by Affirmation, Promise, Description, Sample. (1) Express warranties by the seller are created as follows: (a) Any affirmation of fact or promise made by the seller to the buyer which relates to the goods and becomes part of the basis of the bargain creates an express warranty that the goods shall conform to the affirmation or promise. (b) Any description of the goods which is made part of the basis of the bargain creates an express warranty that the goods shall conform to the description. (c) Any sample or model which is made part of the basis of the bargain creates an express warranty that the whole of the goods shall conform to the sample or model. (2) It is not necessary to the creation of an express warranty that the seller use formal words such as "warrant" or "guarantee" or that he have a specific intention to make a warranty, but an affirmation merely of the value of the goods or a statement purporting to be merely the seller's opinion or commendation of the goods does not create a warranty. Uniform Commercial Code Toolbox About Uniform Laws State Uniform Commercial Codes
perturbation.justification: The modified text contradicts UCC § 2-313 regarding express warranties. The original text provided a specific warranty period and defined 'material defects,' creating a clear express warranty. The changed text replaces 'material defects' with 'to a reasonable extent, be free from defects,' making the standard subjective, and replaces the specific warranty period with 'a duration that Accuray considers suitable,' giving Accuray complete discretion. According to Scraped Law Snippet 1, UCC § 2-313(1)(a), an express warranty is created by 'any affirmation of fact or promise made by the seller to the buyer which relates to the goods and becomes part of the basis of the bargain.' The changed text undermines this affirmation by introducing vagueness and subjectivity regarding the extent of defects covered and the duration of the warranty, thereby contradicting the UCC's requirement for a clear affirmation and potentially denying the customer the protections of an express warranty.

perturbation.original_text: Accuray shall be responsible for installation of Accuray Products at Customer sites.
perturbation.changed_text: Accuray will typically arrange for installation of Accuray Products at Customer sites when feasible.
perturbation.explanation: Replacing 'shall be responsible' with 'will typically arrange' weakens the obligation and 'when feasible' introduces subjective discretion, making installation not guaranteed.
perturbation.law_explanation: California Civil Code § 1797.1 implies certain warranty obligations for assistive devices, potentially including complex medical equipment. If installation is essential for the product to function as intended, making installation discretionary could violate the implied warranty of fitness if the product is not properly installed, and therefore does not function. The Song-Beverly Consumer Warranty Act requires that manufacturers service or repair products under warranty. If a product requires professional installation to function as intended, and the manufacturer fails to install it or subcontracts an improper installation, that could violate the implied and express warranties.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: California Code, CIV 1797.1 skip to content home accessibility FAQ feedback sitemap login x Quick Search: Bill Number Bill Keyword Home Bill Information California Law Publications Other Resources My Subscriptions My Favorites California Law >> >> Code Section Code Section Code: Select Code CONS BPC CIV CCP COM CORP EDC ELEC EVID FAM FIN FGC FAC GOV HNC HSC INS LAB MVC PEN PROB PCC PRC PUC RTC SHC UIC VEH WAT WIC Article: Section: Code: Select Code All BPC CIV CCP COM CORP EDC ELEC EVID FAM FIN FGC FAC GOV HNC HSC INS LAB MVC PEN PROB PCC PRC PUC RTC SHC UIC VEH WAT WIC Section: Keyword(s): Code Search Text Search Up^ << Previous Next >> cross-reference chaptered bills PDF | Add To My Favorites Search Phrase: Code Text Civil Code - CIV DIVISION 3. OBLIGATIONS [1427 - 3273.69] ( Heading of Division 3 amended by Stats. 1988, Ch. 160, Sec. 14. ) PART 4. OBLIGATIONS ARISING FROM PARTICULAR TRANSACTIONS [1738 - 3273.69] ( Part 4 enacted 1872. ) TITLE 1.7. CONSUMER WARRANTIES [1790 - 1797.96] ( Title 1.7 added by Stats. 1970, Ch. 1333. ) CHAPTER 3. Mobilehome Warranties [1797 - 1797.7] ( Chapter 3 added by Stats. 1971, Ch. 1492. ) 1797.1. As used in this chapter: (a) “Contractor” means any person who is a general building contractor within the meaning of Section 7057 of the Business and Professions Code. (b) “Dealer” means any person who is a dealer within the meaning of Section 18002.6 of the Health and Safety Code. (c) “Mobilehome” and “manufactured home” have the meanings, respectively, defined in Sections 18007 and 18008 of the Health and Safety Code. (d) “Substantial defects in materials and workmanship” means defects objectively manifested by broken, ripped, cracked, stained, or missing parts or components, or workmanship resulting in improper function of materials, components, appliances, or systems as installed or manufactured by the contractor, dealer, or manufacturer. (Amended by Stats. 1993, Ch. 458, Sec. 1. Effective January 1, 1994.)
perturbation.justification: The modified text replaces 'shall be responsible' with 'will typically arrange...when feasible,' weakening the original obligation for Accuray to install products. The LLM's law explanation points to California Civil Code § 1797.1 implying warranty obligations, especially if installation is crucial for the product's function. The changed text's ambiguity and conditionality introduce a potential breach of implied warranty if improper installation leads to malfunction. Scraped Law Snippet 1 defines 'Substantial defects in materials and workmanship' which could arise from improper installation, further reinforcing the contradiction. By making installation discretionary, the modified text undermines the implied warranty, leading to a legally questionable scenario.
